name: NetSuite Demo Helper: SC Product Demo

login.instructions: Start the local control panel with npm run control before running this showcase.

segments.title: Open With Codex And Deal Context
segments.objective: Show that the helper is SC-owned, Codex-backed, and working from a saved deal snapshot.
segments.valueStatement: The SC can prepare from real deal inputs while keeping the slower Codex reasoning outside the live customer presentation.
segments.narration: This is NetSuite Demo Helper, a prep workspace built for Solution Consultants. It helps an SC get more value from discovery by turning live deal context into a practical demo plan, SC guide, and intelligence view. For this walkthrough I am using a saved mock ACS deal snapshot, so the app can show realistic prep data without waiting for a fresh Codex run. Codex is still the brain of the workflow: the app verifies that Codex is available, then combines Codex reasoning with the local demo guidelines, audience playbooks, strategy rules, and admin-controlled logic.

segments.title: Prep From A Live Deal Snapshot
segments.objective: Show that the Prep page turns account context, scope, and notes into a structured demo request.
segments.valueStatement: The SC starts with the same deal context they would normally carry in notes, then makes audience, scope, and strategy explicit.
segments.narration: The Prep screen is where the SC shapes the demo before anything is generated. The audience options are here because a prospect, an existing customer, an executive, or a technical stakeholder should not get the same story. The target segment, such as mid-market or enterprise, changes the level of detail, pacing, risk focus, and proof points. The demo scope tells the helper what is in and out of bounds; in this example it is Financials First Services Premium, including SuiteProjects and Fixed Asset Management. The SC can use a demo request when they already know the desired demo topic, pre-demo notes when they want the tool to learn from discovery, or both when the notes and requested flow need to work together.

segments.title: Show The SC Guide
segments.objective: Show the human runbook, persona story, asset prompt, and account setup prompt.
segments.valueStatement: The guide converts deal data into an SC-ready story, talk track, demo order, and preparation checklist.
segments.narration: The SC Guide is the main working output. This is where Codex turns the deal context and local guidelines into a personalized story and runbook: what to show, what to say, what to avoid, and how to keep the flow ordered from the strongest business proof moments to supporting detail. The asset prompt is intentionally lighter now: it is there to help create a persona-led PowerPoint support story. A sample persona deck and a dry-run clickthrough video can be shared separately after this demo.

segments.title: Show Demo Intelligence
segments.objective: Show how the intelligence dashboard helps the SC find vulnerabilities before the customer meeting.
segments.valueStatement: The Intelligence page gives the SC a practical coaching layer: what is strong, what is risky, what is missing, and what to fix before the demo.
segments.narration: The Intelligence tab is where the SC gets a second set of eyes on the demo plan. It answers the questions that matter before the meeting: am I ready, what could weaken this demo, what discovery is missing, which stakeholders are under-served, and where should I slow down because the moment really matters. For an SC, this is valuable because it turns vague prep risk into specific actions. If the notes are missing active countries, local reporting requirements, success metrics, or phase-one scope, the dashboard surfaces that. If the flow is too broad, too technical, or too light on business outcomes, it shows where to tighten the story before the customer ever sees it.

segments.title: Show Intelligence Actions
segments.objective: Show how the SC can use intelligence to ask better discovery questions or improve the guide.
segments.valueStatement: The SC can move from insight to action without copying recommendations into a separate prompt.
segments.narration: Under the intelligence areas, the AI actions turn those findings into next steps. Follow-up questions are discovery-only and are meant to help the SC go back to the account team with sharper questions. The shorten, executive, technical, and custom actions create editable output first. Applying all recommendations is the one-step option when the SC wants the guide improved based on the risks and coaching points already visible on the page.

actions.text: Apply All Recommendations To SC Guide

segments.title: Explain The Manifest
segments.objective: Explain that the manifest is built after the SC guide to make the route rehearseable.
segments.valueStatement: The manifest gives the SC a repeatable route through the browser, reducing demo wandering and rehearsal risk.
segments.narration: After prep, guide, and intelligence, the Dry-Run tab turns the SC Guide page's dry-run creation prompt into a browser-ready run plan. This is not the first thing the SC needs to care about. It is built in so the story can become repeatable: segments, navigation, narration, proof moments, and safe actions. The separate dry-run clickthrough video can show what that route looks like without spending time generating it live.

segments.title: Explain Run And Rehearsal
segments.objective: Show why run controls are included after prep and intelligence.
segments.valueStatement: Run controls help the SC rehearse the route, check timing, prepare the account buffer, and avoid customer-facing surprises.
segments.narration: The Run tab is there for rehearsal and risk reduction. The SC can dry run the manifest, prepare the account buffer, rehearse the clickthroughs, and only then run the live narrated demo. This is also where the stop control matters: if something is running by mistake, the SC has a way to stop the active automation. The main value is still prep, guide, and intelligence; run is the safety layer that helps the SC feel the demo before the customer sees it.

segments.title: Close With Admin Control
segments.objective: Show that local guidance and scoring logic can be updated through Admin.
segments.valueStatement: The admin area lets teams keep the local guidance current so Codex is steered by the right SC standards and playbooks.
segments.narration: The Admin section is where the hardcoded guidance becomes maintainable. Teams can update audience playbooks, strategy logic, scoring guidance, labels, and additional demo sources that should steer how the helper creates guides and scores pre-demo notes. That matters because Codex is powerful, but it should be guided by the team’s own standards. The result is a practical SC prep assistant: faster preparation, better discovery hygiene, stronger demo stories, and fewer missed risks before the customer meeting.
